prompts.title: Basic Worksheet Generator
prompts.template: Act as an experienced high school [SUBJECT] teacher. Create a [NUMBER]-question practice worksheet on [TOPIC] for [GRADE] students. Format it as a clean printable with space between each problem for student work, and include a full answer key with step-by-step worked solutions on a separate page. Make all answers integers only and align to Common Core standards.
prompts.example: Act as an experienced high school Algebra 1 teacher. Create a 12-question practice worksheet on solving two-step equations for 9th graders. Format it as a clean printable with space between each problem for student work, and include a full answer key with step-by-step worked solutions on a separate page. Make all answers integers only and align to Common Core 8.EE.C.7.

prompts.title: Differentiated Worksheet (3 Levels)
prompts.template:
I teach [SUBJECT] to a mixed-ability [GRADE] class. Create THREE versions of a [NUMBER]-problem practice set on [TOPIC]:
- Version A (Below Grade Level): [EASIER DESCRIPTION]
- Version B (On Grade Level): [STANDARD DESCRIPTION]
- Version C (Above Grade Level): [HARDER DESCRIPTION]
Include a complete answer key for each version. Format each as a separate printable section.
prompts.example:
I teach Algebra 1 to a mixed-ability 9th grade class. Create THREE versions of a 6-problem practice set on solving systems of linear equations:
- Version A: substitution only, positive integer solutions, one-step setup
- Version B: substitution and elimination, some negative solutions
- Version C: all three methods, word problems requiring students to set up the system, some fractional coefficients
Include a complete answer key for each version. Format each as a separate printable section.

prompts.title: Answer Key Generator
prompts.template:
I have the following [SUBJECT] problems for [GRADE] students. Create a complete answer key with full step-by-step worked solutions for each problem. Show all work clearly, as a model for students. Problems:
[PASTE YOUR PROBLEMS HERE]
prompts.example:
I have the following Algebra 1 problems for 9th grade students. Create a complete answer key with full step-by-step worked solutions for each problem. Show all work clearly, as a model for students. Problems:
[PASTE YOUR PROBLEMS HERE]

prompts.title: Word Problem Creator
prompts.template: Write [NUMBER] engaging word problems about [MATH TOPIC] for [GRADE] students. Use real-world contexts that teenagers find relevant: [CHOOSE: social media, sports, streaming services, part-time jobs, gaming, food, fashion, music, travel]. Each problem must require students to [MATH SKILL]. Include complete solutions.
prompts.example: Write 6 engaging word problems about linear equations for 8th graders. Use real-world contexts involving social media, sports, and part-time jobs. Each problem must require students to set up and solve a linear equation. Include complete solutions.

prompts.title: Lesson Plan Generator
prompts.template:
Design a [LENGTH]-minute [SUBJECT] lesson plan for [GRADE] students on [TOPIC]. Include:
- Learning objectives aligned to Common Core [STANDARD if known]
- Warm-up activity ([X] min)
- Direct instruction ([X] min)
- Guided practice ([X] min)
- Independent practice ([X] min)
- Exit ticket ([X] min)
List all materials needed. Write at a level where a substitute teacher could follow it.
prompts.example: Design a 60-minute Geometry lesson plan for 10th graders on the Pythagorean theorem and its converse. Include: objectives aligned to Common Core 8.G.B.7, warm-up (5 min), direct instruction (15 min), guided practice (20 min), independent practice (15 min), exit ticket (5 min). List all materials needed.

prompts.title: Exit Ticket Bank
prompts.template:
Create a bank of [NUMBER] exit ticket questions for a [GRADE] [SUBJECT] unit on [TOPIC]. Include:
- [X] conceptual questions (explain, compare, identify, describe)
- [X] procedural questions (calculate, graph, solve, simplify)
- [X] application questions (real-world scenarios, word problems)
Each question should take no more than 3 minutes to answer. Provide answers for all questions.
prompts.example: Create a bank of 15 exit ticket questions for a 10th grade Algebra 2 unit on exponential functions. Include 5 conceptual, 5 procedural, and 5 application questions. Each should take ≤3 minutes. Provide answers for all 15.

prompts.title: Quiz or Test Generator
prompts.template:
Create a [NUMBER]-question [QUIZ/TEST] for [GRADE] [SUBJECT] covering [TOPICS]. Include:
- [X] multiple choice questions (4 answer choices each)
- [X] short answer questions
- [X] free response / show-your-work problems
Write at a [DIFFICULTY] level. Include a complete answer key. Format as a printable student document with name/date/period lines at the top.
prompts.example: Create a 20-question quiz for 9th grade Algebra 1 covering solving one-step and two-step equations. Include 10 multiple choice, 6 short answer, and 4 free response problems. Write at grade level. Include a complete answer key.

prompts.title: Spiral Review / Warm-Up
prompts.template: Create a 5-question spiral review warm-up for a [GRADE] [SUBJECT] class. The questions should review these previously taught topics: [TOPIC 1], [TOPIC 2], [TOPIC 3], [TOPIC 4]. Keep each question quick (2-3 minutes max). Include answers.
prompts.example: Create a 5-question spiral review warm-up for a 9th grade Algebra 1 class. The questions should review: solving one-step equations, evaluating expressions, order of operations, and graphing points. Keep each quick. Include answers.

prompts.title: Real-World Connections
prompts.template: I'm teaching [TOPIC] to [GRADE] students. Give me 5 compelling, real-world examples of where [TOPIC] is used in everyday life or careers that would be relevant and interesting to a [GRADE] student. For each example, suggest one follow-up activity or discussion question I can use in class.
prompts.example: I'm teaching quadratic equations to 10th graders. Give me 5 compelling real-world examples of where quadratics appear in everyday life or careers that would interest a 15-year-old. For each example, suggest one follow-up activity or discussion question.

prompts.title: Parent Email
prompts.template:
I'm a [GRADE] [SUBJECT] teacher. Write a [LENGTH: short/medium] email to parents explaining:
- What students are learning this month: [TOPICS]
- Why these skills matter in real life
- One upcoming assessment/project (due [DATE])
- One specific way parents can support study at home (without needing to know the math)
Keep it friendly, jargon-free, and under [WORD COUNT] words.
prompts.example: I'm a 9th grade Algebra 1 teacher. Write a short email to parents explaining: we're learning linear equations this month, why this matters for real life, an upcoming quiz next Friday, and one tip for supporting their child at home. Under 150 words.

prompts.title: Rubric Generator
prompts.template: Create a standards-aligned rubric for a [ASSIGNMENT TYPE] in [GRADE] [SUBJECT] on [TOPIC]. Include 4 performance levels (Exemplary, Proficient, Developing, Beginning) and evaluate these criteria: [CRITERIA 1], [CRITERIA 2], [CRITERIA 3]. Format as a table.
prompts.example: Create a standards-aligned rubric for a proof project in 10th grade Geometry on triangle congruence. Include 4 performance levels (Exemplary, Proficient, Developing, Beginning) and evaluate: mathematical accuracy, logical reasoning, and presentation/organization. Format as a table.

prompts.title: Concept Explainer (for Teacher)
prompts.template: Explain [MATH CONCEPT] to me as if I need to reteach it from scratch. Include: the key ideas, common student misconceptions, three worked examples progressing from easy to hard, and two real-world analogies I can use with students.
prompts.example: Explain the concept of function notation to me as if I need to reteach it from scratch. Include: the key ideas, common student misconceptions, three worked examples progressing from easy to hard, and two real-world analogies I can use with students.

prompts.title: Student-Friendly Concept Explainer
prompts.template:
Explain [MATH CONCEPT] as if you're talking directly to a [GRADE] student who is struggling with [SPECIFIC DIFFICULTY]. Use:
- Simple, everyday language (no jargon)
- One memorable analogy or real-world comparison
- Two worked examples, step-by-step
- A tip for remembering the key idea
Avoid intimidating language. Be encouraging.
prompts.example: Explain how to find the slope of a line using two points as if talking to a 9th grader who is struggling with fractions. Use simple language, one analogy (like stairs or a ramp), and two worked examples with clear steps. Add a memory tip at the end.

prompts.title: Proof Scaffolding (Geometry)
prompts.template:
Create a two-column proof activity for [GRADE] geometry students proving [THEOREM/STATEMENT].
Create THREE versions:
- Fully Scaffolded: All statements provided; students fill in the reasons only
- Partially Scaffolded: First statement and reason given; every other row left blank
- Independent: Only the given information and diagram description; students write the full proof
Use the same mathematical situation for all three versions. Include a complete answer key.
prompts.example: Create a two-column proof activity for 10th grade geometry students proving that vertical angles are congruent. Create three scaffolded versions as described. Include a full answer key.

prompts.title: Common Misconceptions Guide
prompts.template:
I teach [TOPIC] to [GRADE] students. List the 5 most common misconceptions or mistakes students make with this concept. For each misconception:
- Describe the mistake clearly
- Explain WHY students make this mistake (what they're thinking)
- Give an example of a student's incorrect work
- Show the correct approach
- Suggest a quick classroom activity to address this misconception
prompts.example: I teach solving equations with variables on both sides to 9th graders. List the 5 most common mistakes students make, why they make them, examples of incorrect work, the correct approach, and a quick activity to fix each one.

prompts.title: Interactive HTML Quiz
prompts.template:
Create an interactive HTML quiz with [NUMBER] multiple-choice questions about [TOPIC] for [GRADE] students. Requirements:
- Show whether each answer is correct or incorrect immediately after selection
- Display a brief explanation of the correct answer
- Include a final score at the end
- Make it visually clean and student-friendly
- Use encouraging language for wrong answers ('Not quite! Here's why...')
Questions should cover: [SUBTOPICS]
prompts.example: Create an interactive HTML quiz with 6 multiple-choice questions about the properties of quadrilaterals for 8th graders. Show right/wrong feedback with explanation after each selection. Include a final score. Make it colorful and student-friendly.

prompts.title: Vocabulary / Glossary Builder
prompts.template:
Create a student-friendly math vocabulary guide for a [GRADE] [SUBJECT] unit on [TOPIC]. For each key term:
- Plain-language definition (what it means in simple English)
- The formal/textbook definition
- One visual description or analogy
- One example and one non-example
Terms to include: [LIST YOUR TERMS]
prompts.example: Create a student-friendly math vocabulary guide for a 9th grade Algebra 1 unit on linear functions. For each term include: plain-language definition, formal definition, analogy, example and non-example. Terms: slope, y-intercept, linear equation, rate of change, x-intercept.

prompts.title: Differentiated Explanations (3 Levels)
prompts.template:
Explain [MATH CONCEPT] at three different levels:
1. Concrete level — use physical objects, drawings, or manipulatives (for students who need hands-on)
2. Representational level — use diagrams, tables, or graphs (for visual learners)
3. Abstract level — use symbols and formulas (for students ready for the standard notation)
Each explanation should convey the same core idea but be genuinely different in approach.
prompts.example: Explain the concept of multiplying fractions at three levels: Concrete (use area models or fraction bars), Representational (use diagrams and grids), Abstract (use standard algorithm with symbols). Each should teach the same idea differently.

prompts.title: Parent Letter / Newsletter
prompts.template:
Write a friendly, one-page parent letter about our current math unit. Details:
- Subject/Grade: [SUBJECT, GRADE]
- Topics covered this month: [TOPIC 1], [TOPIC 2]
- Upcoming test/quiz date: [DATE]
- One real-world reason these skills matter
- Three specific ways parents can support their child at home (none require knowing the math)
- Tone: warm, approachable, not intimidating
- Length: under 250 words
prompts.example: Write a friendly parent letter for a 10th grade Geometry class. We're covering triangle congruence and similarity this month. Quiz on Friday the 21st. Include one real-world connection and three home support tips. Under 250 words, warm tone.

prompts.title: Discussion Questions Generator
prompts.template:
I'm starting a lesson on [TOPIC] with [GRADE] students. Create 8 discussion questions that:
- Open with 2 accessible questions anyone can answer (no prior knowledge needed)
- Include 3 questions that connect the math to students' real lives
- Close with 3 higher-order thinking questions (analyze, evaluate, create)
Make them genuinely interesting — questions students might actually want to discuss.
prompts.example: I'm starting a lesson on exponential growth with 10th graders. Create 8 discussion questions: 2 accessible openers, 3 real-life connections (social media followers, viral videos, savings), 3 higher-order questions. Make them interesting.

prompts.title: Reteach Script (Small Group)
prompts.template: A significant number of my [GRADE] students did not understand [CONCEPT] after my first lesson. Write a short reteach script (5-7 minutes) for me to use with a small group. Use a completely different approach than typical textbook instruction. Include: a new analogy, a hands-on or visual component, and two simple practice problems with immediate feedback prompts.
prompts.example: Many of my 9th graders didn't understand solving two-step equations after my first lesson. Write a 5-7 minute reteach script for a small group. Use a different approach than the textbook, include a new analogy (like a balance or mystery box), a visual, and two simple practice problems.

prompts.title: Study Guide Builder
prompts.template:
Create a comprehensive study guide for a [GRADE] [SUBJECT] unit test on [TOPICS]. Include:
- A summary of each key concept (2-3 sentences each)
- Important formulas and when to use them
- Common mistakes to avoid (at least 3)
- 10 practice problems with answers
- A self-check checklist ('I can...' statements)
Format it so students can use it for independent review.
prompts.example: Create a study guide for a 10th grade Geometry unit test on triangles. Include summaries of congruence, similarity, and Pythagorean theorem; key formulas; 3 common mistakes; 10 practice problems with answers; and an 'I can...' checklist.

prompts.title: Socratic Learning Mode (for Students)
prompts.template: I need help understanding [TOPIC]. Don't give me the answer yet. Ask me questions to guide me to figure it out myself.
prompts.example: I need help understanding how to factor trinomials. Don't give me the answer yet. Ask me questions to guide me to figure it out myself.

prompts.title: Lesson Plan (Google Docs-ready)
prompts.template: Create a [LENGTH]-minute lesson plan for [GRADE] [SUBJECT] on [TOPIC]. Format it so I can paste it directly into a Google Doc. Include: learning objective, materials needed, warm-up, instruction, guided practice, independent practice, and closing. Align to California Common Core [STANDARD if known].
prompts.example: Create a 50-minute lesson plan for 8th grade Math on solving systems of equations by substitution. Format for Google Doc. Include: objective, materials, warm-up, instruction, guided practice, independent practice, closing. Align to CA Common Core 8.EE.C.8.

prompts.title: Parent Newsletter Draft
prompts.template: Write a parent newsletter for my [GRADE] [SUBJECT] class. This month we're covering [TOPIC 1] and [TOPIC 2]. The newsletter should: explain what we're learning in plain language, mention our upcoming [TEST/PROJECT] on [DATE], give parents one practical tip to help at home, and be friendly and brief (under 200 words). Format it so it's ready to paste into a Google Doc.
prompts.example: Write a parent newsletter for my 9th grade Algebra 1 class covering linear equations and graphing this month. Mention our upcoming unit test on March 20. Give one practical parent tip. Under 200 words. Ready for Google Doc.

prompts.title: Assignment Description
prompts.template: Write a clear assignment description for a [GRADE] [SUBJECT] class. The assignment is: [BRIEF DESCRIPTION]. Include: what students will do, what to turn in, how it will be graded (brief), and the due date [DATE]. Keep it concise and student-friendly.
prompts.example: Write a clear assignment description for a 10th grade Geometry class. The assignment is a poster project on real-world applications of the Pythagorean theorem. Include what to do, what to submit, brief grading criteria, and due date March 25. Student-friendly.

prompts.title: Quiz Questions Generator
prompts.template: Create [NUMBER] quiz questions for a [GRADE] [SUBJECT] quiz on [TOPIC]. Include [X] multiple choice (with 4 options each), [X] short answer, and [X] true/false. Provide an answer key. Make sure the difficulty is appropriate for [GRADE] students.
prompts.example: Create 12 quiz questions for a 9th grade Algebra 1 quiz on slope and rate of change. Include 6 multiple choice (4 options each), 4 short answer, and 2 true/false. Provide an answer key. Grade-level difficulty.

prompts.title: Standards Alignment
prompts.template: I'm teaching [TOPIC] to [GRADE] students. Which California Common Core Math standards does this topic address? List the standard codes and descriptions. Then suggest 3 specific learning objectives I can write for this lesson.
prompts.example: I'm teaching the Pythagorean theorem to 8th grade students. Which California Common Core Math standards does this address? List the standard codes and descriptions. Suggest 3 specific learning objectives.

prompts.title: Rubric Generator (Google Classroom)
prompts.template: Create a rubric for a [GRADE] [SUBJECT] [ASSIGNMENT TYPE] on [TOPIC]. Use 4 performance levels: Exceeds Standards, Meets Standards, Approaching Standards, Below Standards. Evaluate these criteria: [CRITERION 1], [CRITERION 2], [CRITERION 3].
prompts.example: Create a rubric for a 10th grade Geometry poster project on triangle congruence. Use 4 levels: Exceeds Standards, Meets Standards, Approaching Standards, Below Standards. Evaluate: mathematical accuracy, visual presentation, and real-world connections.

prompts.title: Assignment Creation (Google Classroom)
prompts.template: Create a [SUBJECT] assignment for [GRADE] students on [TOPIC]. The assignment should take about [TIME] to complete. Include: a student-friendly description, clear instructions, and what students need to submit.
prompts.example: Create an Algebra 1 assignment for 9th grade students on graphing linear equations. Should take about 45 minutes. Include: student-friendly description, clear step-by-step instructions, and what to submit in Google Classroom.

prompts.title: Lesson Notes / Teacher Guide (Google Docs)
prompts.template: Write organized teacher notes for a [GRADE] [SUBJECT] lesson on [TOPIC]. Include: key vocabulary with definitions, step-by-step teaching sequence, anticipated student questions and answers, and a quick formative assessment idea.
prompts.example: Write organized teacher notes for a 10th grade Geometry lesson on the Law of Sines. Include: key vocabulary, step-by-step teaching sequence, anticipated student questions and answers, and a quick formative check.

prompts.title: Email / Letter Drafting (Google Docs)
prompts.template: Draft a professional email to parents/guardians about [SITUATION]. Keep it warm, brief, and action-oriented.
prompts.example: Draft a professional email to parents about upcoming state testing — what to expect, how to prepare their student, and what the scores mean. Keep it warm, brief, and action-oriented.

prompts.title: Slide Deck Creation (Google Slides)
prompts.template: Create a slide deck for a [LENGTH]-minute [GRADE] [SUBJECT] lesson on [TOPIC]. Include slides for: title, learning objectives, vocabulary, step-by-step worked example, practice problems, and exit ticket. Keep slides clean with minimal text — use bullet points, not paragraphs.
prompts.example: Create a slide deck for a 50-minute 9th grade Algebra 1 lesson on solving linear inequalities. Include: title slide, objectives, vocabulary (inequality, solution set, boundary), worked example, 3 practice problems, and exit ticket. Minimal text, bullet points.

prompts.title: Visual Explanation Slide (Google Slides)
prompts.template: Create a slide that visually explains [MATH CONCEPT] for [GRADE] students. Use a diagram, color-coding, or step-by-step visual breakdown. Keep text minimal.
prompts.example: Create a slide that visually explains the order of operations (PEMDAS) for 6th grade students. Use color-coding for each operation level, a step-by-step diagram, and keep text minimal.

prompts.title: Google Forms Quiz Creation
prompts.template: Give me 10 multiple-choice questions for a Google Forms quiz on [TOPIC] for [GRADE] students. Include 4 answer options for each, mark the correct answer, and vary the difficulty (4 easy, 4 medium, 2 hard).
prompts.example: Give me 10 multiple-choice questions for a Google Forms quiz on exponential functions for 10th grade students. 4 answer options each, mark correct answers, vary difficulty: 4 easy, 4 medium, 2 hard.

prompts.title: Math Concept Poster
prompts.template: Create a colorful educational poster for a high school math classroom about [CONCEPT]. Include a visual diagram, the key formula, and a simple real-world example. Style: bright, modern, engaging for teenagers. No people. Clean white background or gradient background.
prompts.example: Create a colorful educational poster for a high school math classroom about the Pythagorean Theorem. Include a visual diagram, the formula a²+b²=c², and a simple real-world example. Style: bright, modern, engaging for teenagers. No people. Clean gradient background.

prompts.title: Motivational Math Poster
prompts.template: Create a motivational classroom poster for high school students with the quote '[QUOTE]'. Make it visually striking with bold typography and a math-themed geometric pattern background. Colors: [COLORS]. Style: modern and professional.
prompts.example: Create a motivational classroom poster with the quote 'Do Hard Math'. Make it visually striking with bold typography and a math-themed geometric pattern. Colors: vibrant blues and gold. Style: modern and professional.

prompts.title: Vocabulary / Formula Poster
prompts.template: Create an educational wall poster displaying the [TOPIC] formulas for a high school math classroom. Include: [FORMULA 1], [FORMULA 2], [FORMULA 3]. Use color-coding to highlight different parts of each formula. Style: clean, minimalist, easy to read from across the room. White or light background.
prompts.example: Create an educational wall poster displaying geometry formulas. Include: area of a circle (πr²), Pythagorean theorem (a²+b²=c²), and slope formula. Color-code different parts. Clean, minimalist style, easy to read from the back of the room.

prompts.title: Math Tutoring Flyer
prompts.template: Create a professional flyer advertising FREE math tutoring at [SCHOOL NAME] high school. Include space for: days and times, room number, and a contact email. Style: energetic, welcoming, appealing to high school students. Use bold colors. Add math symbols and geometric shapes as decoration. Leave clear areas where I can add specific text details.
prompts.example: Create a professional flyer advertising FREE math tutoring at a high school. Leave space for days/times, room number, and contact email. Style: energetic, welcoming, bold colors. Math symbols and geometric shapes as decoration.

prompts.title: Math Club Flyer
prompts.template: Create an eye-catching flyer for a high school Math Club. The flyer should look fun and exciting — not intimidating. Include decorative math symbols (∑, π, √, ∞, ÷). Style: modern, colorful, appealing to teens aged 14-18. Leave space at the bottom for meeting time/location details. Horizontal format.
prompts.example: Create a fun, eye-catching flyer for a high school Math Club. Include math symbols (∑, π, √, ∞). Style: modern, colorful, not intimidating, appealing to teens. Space at bottom for meeting details. Horizontal format.

prompts.title: Pi Day Event Flyer
prompts.template: Create a festive flyer for a Pi Day celebration (March 14) at a high school. Include the Pi symbol (π) prominently, the number 3.14159..., and imagery suggesting a fun school event. Make it colorful and celebratory. Style: festive but professional. Leave space for event details.
prompts.example: Create a festive Pi Day flyer for a high school event on March 14. Include π prominently, 3.14159..., fun imagery. Colorful and celebratory. Leave space for event details.

prompts.title: Math Competition Flyer
prompts.template: Create a dramatic, exciting flyer for a high school math competition. Style: like a sports event poster — bold, high-energy, competitive. Include: calculator imagery, trophy imagery, and math symbols. Color scheme: [COLOR SCHEME]. Leave areas for: competition name, date, how to sign up.
prompts.example: Create a dramatic flyer for a high school math competition. Style: sports event poster — bold, high-energy. Include calculator and trophy imagery, math symbols. Color scheme: navy and gold. Leave areas for competition name, date, sign-up info.

prompts.title: Worksheet Header Banner
prompts.template: Create a decorative header banner for a high school math worksheet on [TOPIC]. It should be horizontal (wide and short — like a header strip). Include: the topic name '[TOPIC]' in bold text, math-themed geometric decoration, and space for Name/Date/Period. Colors: [COLOR PREFERENCE]. Style: clean and professional.
prompts.example: Create a decorative header banner for a high school math worksheet on Quadratic Equations. Horizontal, wide and short. Include the title in bold, geometric decoration, and space for Name/Date/Period. Colors: blue and white. Clean and professional.

prompts.title: Unit Cover Page
prompts.template: Create a professional cover page image for a high school [SUBJECT] unit on [TOPIC]. It should look like a textbook chapter opener — visually representing the concept with geometric shapes, graphs, or relevant imagery. Include the unit title '[TOPIC]' in large, clear text. Style: modern textbook design. Colors: [COLOR PREFERENCE].
prompts.example: Create a textbook-style cover page for a high school Geometry unit on Circles. Include geometric circle diagrams, arcs, and chord imagery. Unit title 'Circles' in large text. Modern textbook design. Colors: teal and white.

prompts.title: Formula Reference Card
prompts.template: Create a visual reference card (like a bookmark or index card) for high school students showing key formulas for [TOPIC]. Include: [FORMULA 1], [FORMULA 2], [FORMULA 3]. Use color-coding, clear layout, and small visual icons. Style: compact, easy to study from, clean background. Portrait orientation.
prompts.example: Create a formula reference card for high school Algebra 1 students. Include: slope formula, slope-intercept form, standard form. Color-coded, clear layout, small icons. Compact, easy to study from. Portrait orientation.

prompts.title: Mind Map / Concept Map
prompts.template: Create a visual concept map showing how [MAIN TOPIC] connects to related concepts: [CONCEPT 1], [CONCEPT 2], [CONCEPT 3], [CONCEPT 4]. Use nodes, arrows, and color-coding. Style: clear, educational, suitable for a high school classroom handout.
prompts.example: Create a concept map showing how Linear Functions connects to: slope, y-intercept, graphing, and real-world applications. Use nodes, arrows, color-coding. Clear and educational for a high school classroom handout.

prompts.title: Classroom Door Sign
prompts.template: Create a classroom door sign for a [SUBJECT] class. Include: 'Welcome to [SUBJECT]!' and decorative math symbols or geometric patterns. Style: professional and welcoming. Colors: [YOUR PREFERENCE]. Portrait format.
prompts.example: Create a classroom door sign for a Geometry class. Include 'Welcome to Geometry!' with geometric shapes and math symbols. Professional and welcoming. Colors: navy and gold. Portrait format.

prompts.title: Back-to-School Slide Background
prompts.template: Create a welcoming background image for a Back-to-School night presentation slide. It should feel warm and professional, with subtle math themes (geometric shapes, soft formulas in background). Colors: [SCHOOL COLORS or 'warm blues and greens']. No text — just the background image.
prompts.example: Create a welcoming background for a Back-to-School night slide. Warm and professional, subtle math geometric shapes in background. Colors: warm blues and greens. No text — background image only.

prompts.title: Quick Poster Template
prompts.template: Create a [STYLE] classroom poster about [TOPIC] for high school students. Include [KEY ELEMENT]. Colors: [COLORS]. No people.
prompts.example: Create a minimalist classroom poster about the Pythagorean Theorem for high school students. Include a right triangle diagram and the formula. Colors: deep blue and white. No people.

prompts.title: Quick Flyer Template
prompts.template: Create a [STYLE] flyer for [EVENT] at a high school. Leave space for [DETAILS TO ADD LATER]. Colors: [COLORS]. [ORIENTATION] format.
prompts.example: Create a modern, energetic flyer for a Math Night event at a high school. Leave space for date, time, and location. Colors: bright orange and dark blue. Vertical format.

prompts.title: Math Graph / Visualization Generator
prompts.template: Write Python code using matplotlib to graph [MATH FUNCTION OR CONCEPT]. Label the axes, title the graph '[TITLE]', and add a legend. Make it suitable for a [GRADE] classroom display. Show the code and then explain what each part does in simple terms.
prompts.example: Write Python code to graph y = 2x + 3 and y = -x + 5 on the same coordinate plane. Show where they intersect. Label both lines and the intersection point. Title it 'Systems of Equations'. Explain the code simply.

prompts.title: Interactive Math Tool Creator
prompts.template: Write Python code for a simple [TOOL TYPE] for [GRADE] math students. It should [DESCRIBE BEHAVIOR]. Keep it simple enough that a math teacher with no coding experience could run it.
prompts.example: Write Python code for a simple equation solver for 9th grade math students. A student types in a linear equation like '2x + 5 = 11' and the program shows the step-by-step solution. Keep the code simple and well-commented.

prompts.title: Automated Answer Checker
prompts.template: Write Python code that takes a list of [MATH TOPIC] problems and a list of student answers, compares them to the correct answers, and outputs a score and which problems the student got wrong. Here are the problems and correct answers: [PASTE YOUR PROBLEMS AND ANSWERS]
prompts.example: Write Python code that takes a list of two-step equation problems and student answers, compares to correct answers, and outputs score + which were wrong. Problems: [2x+3=11 → x=4, 3x-5=10 → x=5, etc.]

prompts.title: Data Analysis for Statistics
prompts.template: Write Python code to [STATISTICAL TASK] using this dataset: [PASTE DATA OR DESCRIBE IT]. Show the calculation step by step and output: the result, a brief interpretation in plain English, and a visualization if appropriate.
prompts.example: Write Python code to create a scatter plot with line of best fit for this data: hours studied vs. test score: x=[1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8], y=[55,60,65,70,72,80,85,90]. Calculate and display the correlation coefficient.

prompts.title: Lesson Content with Code Examples
prompts.template: I'm teaching [TOPIC] to [GRADE] students. Write me a short explanation of the concept, followed by Python code that demonstrates it visually or computationally. Then write 3 questions students could answer after seeing the code run.
prompts.example: I'm teaching the concept of slope to 8th graders. Write a short explanation, then Python code graphing 3 lines with different slopes for visual comparison. Then write 3 discussion questions.

prompts.title: Jupyter Notebook Lesson Plan
prompts.template: Create a Jupyter Notebook outline for a [GRADE] [SUBJECT] lesson on [TOPIC]. Include: a text explanation of the concept, Python code cells that demonstrate the math, practice problems where students fill in the code, and 3 reflection questions. Write in a format that a math teacher with no coding background can follow.
prompts.example: Create a Jupyter Notebook outline for an 8th grade Math lesson on linear functions. Include: text explanation, Python code cells showing graphs, fill-in practice cells for students, and 3 reflection questions. Written for a teacher with no coding background.

prompts.title: Word Problems (Copilot Chat)
prompts.template: Write 5 word problems about [TOPIC] for [GRADE] students using real-world contexts. Include full solutions.
prompts.example: Write 5 word problems about systems of linear equations for 9th grade students using real-world contexts like budgeting, sports, or food prices. Include full solutions.

chatgpt.name: ChatGPT
chatgpt.tagline: Best for worksheets, lesson plans & content generation
chatgpt.free: Free plan available; educator plan at chatgpt.com/plans/k12-teachers

claude.name: Claude
claude.tagline: Best for explanations, proofs & interactive HTML tools

gemini.name: Gemini
gemini.tagline: Best for Google Classroom, Docs, Slides & image generation
gemini.free: Free with Google account; included with Google Workspace for Education

copilot.name: GitHub Copilot
copilot.tagline: Best for Python graphs, visualizations & verified math
